title: Cuban black beans
subtitle: Slow-simmered black beans with sofrito and cumin
excerpt: Black beans cooked in a Cuban-style sofrito of green pepper, onion, garlic, and cumin with a splash of vinegar to brighten. The essential side dish of Cuban cooking, eaten with white rice.
sourceNotes: Cuban black beans (frijoles negros) are the cornerstone of Cuban cooking, eaten daily with white rice. The traditional method uses dried black beans soaked overnight and cooked for hours; this recipe uses tinned black beans for speed without significant loss in quality. The Cuban sofrito base of green pepper, onion, garlic, and cumin gives the beans their character. A splash of cider vinegar added at the end is the Cuban finishing touch that brightens the dish.

recipe.freezeNotes: Freeze in portions. Reheat gently with a splash of water.
recipe.batchNotes: Doubles or triples easily. Keeps in the fridge for 5 days.
recipe.makeAheadNotes: Better the next day once the flavours have deepened.

content.text: Cuban black beans are cooked twice: first the sofrito base is built, then the beans are added and simmered until the sauce becomes thick and glossy. The sofrito is the work — the combination of green pepper, onion, garlic, cumin, and oregano is specifically Cuban and distinct from the tomato-heavy sofrito of other Latin cuisines. The vinegar at the end is not optional; it balances the earthiness of the beans and the sweetness of the peppers.
Tinned black beans are perfectly good here — the flavour comes from the sofrito, not the beans. If you want to cook from dried, soak overnight and boil for 90 minutes before starting the sofrito.

items.prepNote: tinned, drained — or 300 g dried, soaked overnight

content.text: Heat the {{olive-oil}} of olive oil in a saucepan over medium heat. Add the {{onion}} onion and {{pepper-green}} green pepper. Cook for 8 minutes until soft. Add the {{garlic}} of garlic, {{cumin-ground}} of cumin, and {{oregano-dried}} of oregano. Stir for 1 minute.

Add the {{black-beans}} of drained black beans, {{bay-leaves}} of bay, and 300 ml of cold water. Season with {{sea-salt-fine}} of salt. Bring to a boil, then reduce to a simmer. Using the back of a spoon, crush about a quarter of the beans against the side of the pan — this thickens the broth without adding starch. Simmer uncovered for 20 minutes until the sauce is thick and glossy. Add the {{cider-vinegar}} of cider vinegar, stir, and taste for seasoning. Remove the bay leaves before serving.

items.cause: Not enough beans crushed; lid on throughout
items.fix: Crush a third of the beans with the back of a spoon. Simmer without a lid to evaporate excess liquid.

items.cause: Cumin and oregano not bloomed in oil; vinegar omitted
items.fix: Cook the spices for 1 minute in the oil before adding anything else. The vinegar at the end is essential — add it and taste the difference.
Start with 10 ml and add more to taste. The vinegar should be background brightness, not prominent acidity.
Tinned beans are already fully cooked and need only 15–20 minutes in the sofrito. Longer and they fall apart completely.

content.text: Cuban black beans appear at every Cuban meal as a side beside white rice — together they make arroz con frijoles, the combination that sustains daily life in Cuba and in the Cuban diaspora. The beans are spooned over the rice and the two are mixed at the table or eaten separately. They also combine with rice during cooking to make moros y cristianos. Cuban food without black beans is inconceivable; this is the dish that underpins the entire cuisine.
